But since last month, the fund has been rejected from the $48.35 resistance level and the fact that the fund's stocks are in overbought areas increases the possibility that the fund may be under pressure in the near future. So even though I think the fund will tend to rise in the long term, I do not think it is in the buy area at the moment. That's why my stance is "hold" for now.

data.title: Venture Global: Rapidly Closing The Valuation Gap With Cheniere
data.description:
Venture Global operates and develops LNG export facilities along the Gulf Coast in Louisiana. The company uses modular liquefaction trains to reduce capital costs. The company is expanding its capacity at breakneck speed. June 2 FERC gave the green light to go ahead with its CP2 project. To quote:

If completed, CP2 would be the single largest LNG export facility in the U.S. and could make Venture Global (NYSE:VG) the largest U.S. LNG company with 66.5M tons/year, surpassing Cheniere Energy's (LNG) expected 60M tons/year of capacity by that time.

Just one day later, Venture Global PR’ed that the work is ongoing (emphasis mine):

Venture Global said CP2 is well advanced in engineering, procurement and contracting; separate from the site work that has started, the project already has significant off-site work underway on modules and equipment.

These releases have had a profound effect on the company's share price, with the stock up 28% since June 2:

I’ve highlighted VG as an interesting company in PDX: High Income And Opportunistic Bets At A Steep Discount. The article is about a closed-end fund that allocated an estimated ~20% of its capital into VG. In that article, I also discuss litigation that the company is dealing with. In the long-run, if the operations operate as planned, I don’t think it will matter much. The valuation will mostly depend on the margin the company is getting on its 20-year contracts and the soundness of capital allocation once the cash starts rolling in.

The company is very similar to Cheniere Energy, which is why I compared it to that company in my prior article. The idea behind the small modular units is that construction is cheaper, compared to Cheniere, and faster. The latter seems borne out by the speed at which the company has been moving in the last few days.

In terms of market cap, VG is still quite a lot behind LNG, meaning there’s upside potential. In terms of EV they’re somewhat closer, though:

VG is in full expansion mode, while LNG is only slowly expanding. In effect, LNG is far more mature of an operation. However, I’d expect (if things go right) for VG to grow into the LNG profile. Both are in a great business. The growth profile is clearly reflected in the forward EV/EBITDA, where the company’s trade close at 10x and 12x. Meanwhile, current EV/EBITDAs are 24.5x and 11.5x. It is also borne out by the higher EV/sales and P/sales metrics.

The balance sheet shows both have very high debt levels. However, this is supported by the business model, which is to sell production (for the most part) through long-term contracts (20-years). The latest data is VG having long-term contracts for 39.25 MTPA.

I expect many more will be added to that soon, as management said the following on the latest earnings call:

We're very active in a significant number of negotiations for long-term contracts at this point, mostly all 20-year terms. And so we expect to be executing and reporting on multiple 20-year contracts in the incoming quarters. And we're really pleased with the demand and where we stand in those negotiations. So, we -- the market, the investors in our company should expect to see announcements for more 20-year deals.

Europe has been cutting back its reliance on Russian gas. In 2021, it got 45% of its gas from Russia. That’s down to 19%. The current roadmap to fully cut dependency on Russian gas plans to cut this 19% down by another 1/3rd at the end of this year. Then it is supposed to go to zero by the end of 2027. LNG is a growth market to begin with but this should be a significant tailwind to the industry and Europe is likely to embrace LNG imports as these support the desire to cut reliance on Russia but also help with the trade tensions between Europe and the U.S. VG could ultimately exceed LNG's export capacity. LNG is a growth market and Europe is desperate to replace Russian supply. There is still quite a way to go before VG is valued in-line with Cheniere while they are very comparable. Even though the stock has strongly responded to the news events, I believe it is a strong buy for now while it narrows this valuation gap.
